plainMeaning: Before confessing the twelve vows, the layperson first renews right faith — rejecting every kind of false belief and wrong worship — and then examines and repents the first vow, non-violence, listing the careless ways daily life may have harmed living beings.
recitation: (moṭā atiyār) icchaṁ śrāvaka-taṇo dharme śrī samyaktva-mūla bāra vrata bhaṇīe. icchaṁ arihaṁta deva, susādhu guru, jina-praṇīta dharma bhāvataḥ samakita pratipāluṁ. dravyato laukika lokottara deva-gata, guru-gata, parva-gata mithyātva caturvidha bhaṇīe. hari, hara, brahmā, sūrya, iṁdra, caṁdra, graha, gotraja, gaṇeśa, dikpāla, kṣetrapāla, skaṁda, kapila, buddha, hanumaṁta, yakṣa, rākṣasa bhakti muktidāyaka bhaṇī ārādhīe — te laukika deva-gata mithyātva. caraka, parivrājaka, kaula, kāpālika, dvija, tāpasa saṁsāra-tāraka bhaṇī mānīe — te laukika guru-gata mithyātva. para-parigṛhīta jina-biṁba, vairoṭyā, brahma, śāṁti pramukha jaina deva devī-taṇuṁ je deva-buddhe pūjana te lokottara deva-gata mithyātva. pāsatthā, usannā, kuśīla, saṁsakta, ahācchaṁda, ninhava, boṭika, dravya-liṁgī-taṇo viṣe je guru-buddhe pūjana te lokottara guru-gata mithyātva. e caturvidha mithyātva yathāśakte pariharuṁ. e śrī samakita-taṇā pāṁca atiyār śodhuṁ. śaṁkā, kaṁkhā, vitigicchā, para-pāsaṁḍi para-saṁsā, para-pāsaṁḍi saṁthuo. śaṁkā — jīvājīvādika nava-tattva-māṁhe eke tattva-taṇo mana saṁdeha dharyo hoya, athavā deva-guru-dharma siddhāṁta-taṇo mana saṁdeha dharyo hoya. kāṁkṣā — apara dharma-taṇo abhilāṣa dharyo hoya, athavā sarve dharma sarakhā lekhavyā hoya. vitigicchā — dharma-taṇā phala-pratye mana saṁdeha dharyo hoya, athavā mala-malīna gātra tapodhana, tapodhanā dekhī dugaṁchā kīdhī hoya. para-pāsaṁḍi para-śaṁsā — para-darśanī-taṇī atiśaya vidyā-khyāti dekhī praśaṁsā kīdhī hoya. para-pāsaṁḍi saṁthuo — para-darśanī-śuṁ saṁstava, paricaya, iṣṭa-goṣṭī, aṁtaraṁga prīti, bhakti, dāna, ālāpādika kīdhāṁ karāvyāṁ hoya. para-tīrthe sva-vaśapaṇe gayā hoie. snāna, dāna, homa, mahotsava kīdhāṁ karāvyāṁ hoya tathā saṁkrāṁti grahaṇa-snāna, dānādika karma samācaryāṁ hoya. hoḷī, paḍalī pūjī hoya, pīpala, tulasī, pāṇī ghālyāṁ hoya. nadī, kuṁḍa, pramukha laukika-tīrthe dharma-buddhe snāna kīdhuṁ hoya. ādityavāre ekādaśī bhaṇī tapa kīdhuṁ hoya. loka-pravāhe deva-devī bhaṇī yātrā ujāṇī mānī hoya. kanyāhala līdhuṁ hoya, nīla, tulasī paraṇāvyā hoya. śrāddha saṁvatsarī jamyā hoie. ajñā-paḍavo, baleva, bhāibīja, akhātrīja, viṇāyaga-cotha, nāga-pāṁcama, jhīlaṇā-chaṭṭha, śīla-sātama, dhru-āṭhama, māhī-noma, ahava-daśamī, vijayā-daśamī, bhīma-ekādaśī, vaccha-bāraśī, dhana-teraśī, śiva-caturdaśī, paitṛkī-amāvāsyā, māhī-pūnama tathā saṁkrāṁti, grahaṇa, vyatipāta, vaidhṛta pramukha laukika-parva ane aṣṭamī, caturdaśī, pūrṇimā, amāvāsyā, tathā paryuṣaṇa-parva-taṇī trīja, cotha, pāṁcama ṭāḷī anere divase parva-buddhe tapa kīdhuṁ hoya. aneruṁ je kāṁi jina-vacana virādhyuṁ hoya. aneruṁ samyaktva-viṣe pakṣa-divasa-māṁhe jiko koi sūkṣma, bādara atiyār huo hoya te savi huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ. 1. bāra-vrata māṁhe paheluṁ prāṇātipāta viramaṇa vrata, sthūla be-iṁdriyādika trasa jīva niraparādha upeta-karaṇa saṁkalpī karī haṇavā niyama, āraṁbhe jayaṇā. e pahelā prāṇātipāta viramaṇa vrata-taṇā pāṁca atiyār śodhuṁ. baṁdhe, vahe, chavicchede, aibhāre, bhattapāṇavucchee. dvipada, catuṣpada pratye nibiḍa-baṁdha bāṁdhyā hoya. rīsa-vaśe ḍhora, śvāna, māṁjāra, dāsa, kumāra, choru, vācharu pratye gāḍho prahāra dīdho hoya. chavicchheda — karṇa kaṁbalādika-taṇo cheda kīdho hoya. cauka-ḍī kuṁḍalī paḍāvī hoya. balīvarda nathāvyā hoya. atibhāra āropaṇa — poṭhiyā, vahitrā, ūṁṭa, balada, khara, vesara-ne atibhāra āropyo hoya. bhakta-pāna-vyavacchheda — kuṭuṁba-nāyaka huṁte bhūkhyāṁ, tarasyāṁ, glāna, vṛddha, choru, vācharu-taṇī sāra-saṁbhāla kīdhī na hoya. laheṇo deve ajimavo soṁha dīdho hoya, lāṁghaṇā pāḍī hoya. khāla vāhyā hoya. śalyāṁ dhāna daḷāvyāṁ bharaḍāvyāṁ hoya. galaṇuṁ vaheluṁ āpyuṁ na hoya. nīla-phūla-nī jayaṇā kīdhī na hoya. dhābā māṁḍyā hoya. kou ghātī hoya. pūṁje āga dīdhī hoya. varāse dīvo ughāḍo mūkyo hoya. vāśīgāre līṁpaṇuṁ kīdhuṁ karāvyuṁ hoya. varasāle khātra calāvyā hoya. taḍake māṁkaḍa pakhocyā hoya. rātre snāna, aṁghoḷa kīdhuṁ hoya. ākhuṁ phophala dāṁte bhāṁgyuṁ hoya. gharanā māṇasane jayaṇā viśe śikhāmaṇā dīdhī na hoya. aneruṁ e pahelā prāṇātipāta-viramaṇa-vrata viṣe pakṣa divasa-māṁhe jiko koi sūkṣma, bādara atiyār huo hoya, te savi huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati moṭā 'big, great' + atiyār (the book's spelling of atichāra)
wordByWord.notes: A heading naming the long confession, distinguishing it from the laghu (short) atiyār.

wordByWord.gloss: I assent / 'so be it' — I willingly undertake
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit icchaṁ from Skt. icchā 'wish, assent'
wordByWord.notes: Ritual word of acceptance; recurs to open the act of faith.
Declares samyaktva the foundation of all twelve vows.
Recurs as the formulaic 'we now declare'.

wordByWord.gloss: the Arihant (liberated, omniscient one) as God
wordByWord.notes: Names the only true deva for a Jain.

wordByWord.gloss: the Dharma expounded by the Jina
outwardly / in substance (as opposed to inner feeling)
wordByWord.notes: Pairs with bhāvataḥ; introduces the analysis of mithyātva to be renounced.
First axis of the four-fold mithyātva.
A third sub-axis, expanded later in the festival catalogue.
First of the worldly deities a śrāvaka may wrongly worship.
Closes the worldly-deity list (marked with a footnote bar in the book).
The wrong belief: that these deities can grant mokṣa.

wordByWord.etymology: Old-Gujarati postposition bhaṇī 'for the sake of, as'

wordByWord.notes: First of the non-Jain teacher-types (laukika guru-gata).

wordByWord.gloss: as deliverers across the ocean of rebirth
wordByWord.etymology: saṁsāra + tāraka 'one who ferries across' + bhaṇī 'deeming'
wordByWord.notes: The mistaken belief about these teachers.
Begins lokottara deva-gata: even a Jina-image, if held/worshipped in a non-Jain manner.

wordByWord.etymology: name of a Jain kṣetra/yakṣa deity

wordByWord.gloss: with the notion that they are (true) gods
wordByWord.notes: The error is the attitude, not the object.

wordByWord.gloss: a lax monk (pārśvastha, one who has fallen aside)
wordByWord.notes: First of the fallen/false-ascetic types (lokottara guru-gata).

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ← Skt. yathā-chanda 'acting on one's own whim'
wordByWord.notes: These five are the pañca-kuśīla (fallen ascetics).

wordByWord.gloss: Boṭika (a sectarian, traditionally the Digambara schism)
wordByWord.notes: Named per the Śvetāmbara sectarian framing.

wordByWord.gloss: of one who wears the ascetic mark only outwardly
wordByWord.etymology: dravya 'mere outer substance' + liṅgī 'bearer of the mark' + gen.
wordByWord.notes: An ascetic in form but not in inner discipline.

wordByWord.gloss: with the notion that they are (true) gurus
to the best of my ability

wordByWord.notes: Verbatim orthography preserved per fidelity rules.
Recurs as the formula introducing each vow's five slips.

wordByWord.gloss: wavering / disgust at the fruit of dharma

wordByWord.notes: Second object of doubt under śaṅkā.
Object of vitigicchā (doubt of dharma's efficacy).
Describes an ascetic whose bodily austerity the layperson scorns.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit dugaṁchā ← Skt. jugupsā 'aversion' + kīdhī 'done' + hoya

wordByWord.gloss: of an adherent of another doctrine
may have done or caused to be done
wordByWord.etymology: kīdhāṁ 'done' + karāvyāṁ 'caused to be done' (causative) + hoya
wordByWord.notes: The done/caused pair widens culpability; recurs throughout.
Begins the catalogue of popular observances.

wordByWord.gloss: may have performed gift-giving and such acts
wordByWord.etymology: dāna + ādi + karma 'act' + sam-ā-√car 'to perform' + hoya

wordByWord.gloss: may have worshipped paḍalī (a folk shrine/object)
wordByWord.notes: Exact referent dialectal; a local folk-worship.

wordByWord.gloss: the pīpal tree, the tulsī plant

wordByWord.notes: Bathing becomes a fault only when done as religion.

wordByWord.gloss: for the sake of the Ekādaśī (11th-day) observance
for the sake of gods and goddesses
may have undertaken a pilgrimage or picnic-fair
may have taken part in 'kanyā-hala' (a folk bride-rite)
wordByWord.notes: Local custom; precise referent uncertain — see notes.

wordByWord.gloss: may have ceremonially 'married' the nīla-plant or tulsī
wordByWord.etymology: nīla (a plant) + tulasī + paraṇāv- 'to wed (causative)' + hoya

wordByWord.gloss: may have dined at a śrāddha death-anniversary feast
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. śrāddha 'ancestral rite' + saṁvatsarī 'annual' + Guj. jamyā 'ate' + hoie

wordByWord.gloss: the festival of the first lunar day (paḍavo)
eclipse, Vyatīpāta and Vaidhṛti (inauspicious astronomical yogas)

wordByWord.notes: Summarizes the whole catalogue as laukika.

wordByWord.gloss: the 8th, 14th, full-moon and new-moon (the Jain parva-days)
wordByWord.notes: Now naming the legitimate Jain tithis, to be kept.
Key word: the fault is fasting with religious intent on days OTHER than these proper Jain ones.

wordByWord.gloss: with the notion of (its being) a holy-day
may have transgressed the Jina's word
wordByWord.etymology: jina + vacana 'word' + Skt. vi-√rādh 'to violate' + hoya

wordByWord.notes: Per the substitution rule, read 'four months'/'year' in longer observances.
The recurring subtle–gross pair at every seal.
The mana–vacana–kāyā triad sealing every section.

wordByWord.gloss: may my wrong-doing be undone / in vain
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit micchā 'false' + mi 'mine' + dukkaḍaṁ 'ill-deed' (Skt. mithyā me duṣkṛtam)

wordByWord.notes: Book numbering; the period is part of the text.

wordByWord.gloss: the vow of abstaining from harming life

wordByWord.notes: Marks it as an aṇuvrata, not a monk's full mahāvrata.
Defines the scope of the lay non-violence vow.
As opposed to immobile sthāvara one-sensed beings.
The vow guards the harmless; qualifies the resolve.
The vow forbids intentional killing of innocent mobile beings.
The lay exception: minimize harm where total avoidance is impossible.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ← Skt. bhakta-pāna-vyavaccheda 'cutting off food and water'
wordByWord.notes: 5th atichāra. These five are the canonical Prakrit formula, glossed below.

wordByWord.gloss: may have tied (them) in tight bonds

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. ḍhora; Skt. śvan; Guj. māṁjāra 'cat'
Skt. dāsa 'servant', kumāra 'boy'; Guj. choru 'child', vāchru 'calf'

wordByWord.gloss: may have dealt a heavy blow
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. gāḍho 'severe' + Skt. prahāra 'blow' + dīdho 'gave' + hoya

wordByWord.gloss: may have cut the ear, dewlap and the like
wordByWord.etymology: karṇa 'ear' + kambala 'dewlap' + ādi + gen. + cheda 'cutting' + kīdho + hoya

wordByWord.gloss: may have had (the animal) branded/marked with a ring or coil
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. cauka-ḍī / kuṁḍalī 'ring-mark' + paḍāvī 'caused to be put' + hoya
wordByWord.notes: A bodily mutilation; exact tool dialectal.

wordByWord.gloss: may have had oxen nose-roped (pierced for a nose-rein)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. balīvarda 'ox' + Guj. nathāv- 'to fix a nose-rope (nathh)' + hoya

wordByWord.notes: Heading for the gloss of aibhāre.

wordByWord.token: પોઠિયા, વહિત્રા, ઊંટ, બલદ, ખર, વેસરને
wordByWord.translit: poṭhiyā, vahitrā, ūṁṭa, balada, khara, vesara-ne
wordByWord.gloss: onto pack-bullocks, draught-beasts, camels, oxen, donkeys, mules
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. poṭhiyā 'pack-ox', vahitrā 'beast of burden', ūṁṭa 'camel', balada 'ox', khara 'donkey', vesara 'mule'

wordByWord.gloss: may have loaded an excessive burden

wordByWord.notes: Heading for the gloss of bhattapāṇavucchee.

wordByWord.gloss: being the head of the household

wordByWord.token: ભૂખ્યાં, તરસ્યાં, ગ્લાન, વૃદ્ધ, છોરુ, વાછરુતણી
wordByWord.translit: bhūkhyāṁ, tarasyāṁ, glāna, vṛddha, choru, vācharu-taṇī
wordByWord.gloss: of the hungry, thirsty, sick, aged, children and calves
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. bhūkhyā 'hungry', tarasyā 'thirsty'; Skt. glāna 'ill', vṛddha 'old'; choru, vāchru + gen.
Guj. sāra-saṁbhāla 'care, looking-after' + kīdhī + na 'not' + hoya
wordByWord.notes: Negligence as a form of starving them.

wordByWord.token: લહેણો દેવે અજિમવો સોંહ દીધો હોય
wordByWord.translit: laheṇo deve ajimavo soṁha dīdho hoya
wordByWord.gloss: may have made a debtor swear an oath not to eat (till he pays)
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. laheṇo 'debt/creditor' + deve 'debtor' + ajimavo 'not to dine' + soṁha 'oath' + dīdho + hoya

wordByWord.gloss: may have forced (someone) to go without food
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. lāṁghaṇa 'fasting, skipping a meal' + pāḍī 'caused' + hoya

wordByWord.gloss: may have flayed/skinned (an animal) or driven it hard
may have had infested/verminous grain milled or coarsely ground
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. śalyāṁ 'worm-eaten' + dhāna 'grain' + daḷāv- 'have milled' + bharaḍāv- 'have coarse-ground' + hoya
wordByWord.notes: Grinding grain with living organisms in it.

wordByWord.gloss: may not have supplied a water-strainer in good time
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. galaṇuṁ 'filter-cloth' + vaheluṁ 'early' + āpyuṁ 'gave' + na + hoya

wordByWord.gloss: may not have taken care regarding mould/fungus (nīla-phūla)
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. nīl-phūl 'blue-mould, fungus' + gen. + jayaṇā 'care' + kīdhī + na + hoya

wordByWord.gloss: may have set fire to a heap (of refuse/grass)
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. pūṁjo 'heap, sweepings' + āga 'fire' + dīdhī + hoya
wordByWord.notes: Burning a heap destroys the creatures in it.

wordByWord.gloss: may have left a lamp burning uncovered
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. varāse (in the rains/eaves?) + dīvo 'lamp' + ughāḍo 'uncovered' + mūkyo 'left' + hoya
wordByWord.notes: Insects perish in an open flame; varāse reading uncertain.

wordByWord.gloss: may have plastered (a floor) with stale cow-dung / done or had it done
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. vāsī 'stale' + gār 'dung-mortar' + līṁpaṇuṁ 'plastering' + kīdhuṁ/karāvyuṁ + hoya

wordByWord.gloss: may have dug/turned the soil during the rains
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. varasāle 'in the monsoon' + khātra 'digging' + calāvyā 'plied' + hoya
wordByWord.notes: The rainy season teems with life; digging then is especially injurious.

wordByWord.gloss: may have thrown/exposed bed-bugs to the sun
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. taḍako 'sunshine' + māṁkaḍa 'bug' + pakhoc- 'to fling/spread' + hoya

wordByWord.gloss: may have bathed or washed at night
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. rātri 'night' + snāna; Guj. aṁghoḷa 'bath' + kīdhuṁ + hoya
wordByWord.notes: Night-bathing risks unseen harm to creatures.

wordByWord.gloss: may have cracked a whole areca-nut with the teeth
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. ākhuṁ 'whole' + phophala 'areca/betel-nut' + dāṁte 'with teeth' + bhāṁgyuṁ 'broke' + hoya
wordByWord.notes: A whole nut may harbour life; biting it whole is careless.

wordByWord.token: ઘરના માણસને જયણા વિશે શિખામણા દીધી ન હોય
wordByWord.translit: gharanā māṇasane jayaṇā viśe śikhāmaṇā dīdhī na hoya
wordByWord.gloss: may not have instructed the household in careful guarding (jayaṇā)
wordByWord.etymology: Guj. ghar 'house' + māṇasa 'person' + jayaṇā + viśe 'about' + śikhāmaṇa 'instruction' + dīdhī + na + hoya
wordByWord.notes: Failing to teach the family non-violent care is itself a slip.

literalTranslation: (The Great Transgression-confession.) I assent: in the layperson's discipline I declare the twelve vows rooted in right faith. I assent: I will inwardly uphold right faith — the Arihant as God, the true ascetic as Guru, the Dharma taught by the Jina. Outwardly I now declare the four-fold false belief: worldly and transcendent, regarding deities and teachers (and holy-days). If we have worshipped Hari, Hara, Brahmā, Sūrya, Indra, the Moon, the planets, the clan-deity, Gaṇeśa, the Dikpālas, Kṣetrapāla, Skanda, Kapila, the Buddha, Hanumān, yakṣas, rākṣasas with devotion, deeming them givers of liberation — that is worldly deity-false-belief. If we have honoured Carakas, wandering renunciants, Kaulas, Kāpālikas, brahmins, austerity-ascetics as deliverers across rebirth — that is worldly teacher-false-belief. Worshipping a Jina-image appropriated by non-Jains, or Vairoṭyā, Brahma, Śānti and other Jain gods and goddesses, with the notion that they are (saving) gods — that is transcendent deity-false-belief. Worshipping, with the notion that they are (true) gurus, a lax monk, a slack monk, one of bad conduct, an entangled one, a self-willed one, a doctrine-denier, a Boṭika, or a merely outward ascetic — that is transcendent teacher-false-belief. This four-fold false belief I renounce to the best of my ability. Now I examine the five transgressions of right faith: doubt, craving, wavering-disgust, praise of other sects, intimacy with other sects. Doubt — if a doubt has been held in the mind about any of the nine reals beginning with soul and non-soul, or about the deva–guru–dharma doctrine. Craving — if a longing for another religion has been held, or all religions reckoned alike. Wavering — if doubt has been held about the fruit of dharma, or if, seeing ascetics with dirt-grimed limbs, disgust has been felt. Praise of other sects — if, seeing the great learning and fame of another's creed, praise has been offered. Intimacy with other sects — if familiar praise, acquaintance, fond talk, inner affection, devotion, gifts, conversation and the like have been done or caused with adherents of other creeds. If I have gone of my own accord to a non-Jain holy site; performed or caused bathing, alms-giving, fire-oblation, festivals, or eclipse- and solstice-baths and gift-rites. If I have worshipped at Holī or a paḍalī, poured water (in offering) to pīpal or tulsī. If I have bathed with religious intent at rivers, tanks and other worldly sites. If I have fasted for Ekādaśī on a Sunday. If, swept by custom, I have made a pilgrimage or fair for some god or goddess. If I have taken part in the kanyā-hala rite, or 'wed' the nīla-plant or tulsī. If I have dined at a śrāddha annual-feast. If — excepting the eighth, fourteenth, full-moon, new-moon, and the third, fourth, fifth of Paryuṣaṇa — I have fasted with a holy-day notion on the festival-days of the first lunar day, Baleva, Bhāī-bīj, Akhātrīj, Vināyaka-caturthī, Nāga-pañcamī, Jhīlaṇā-ṣaṣṭhī, Śīla-saptamī, Dhru-aṣṭamī, Māhī-navamī, Ahava-daśamī, Vijayā-daśamī, Bhīma-ekādaśī, Vatsa-dvādaśī, Dhana-trayodaśī, Śiva-caturdaśī, ancestral new-moon, Māhī-pūrṇimā, and at solstice-transits, eclipses, Vyatīpāta, Vaidhṛti and other worldly festivals. Whatever else of the Jina's word has been transgressed, whatever subtle or gross transgression regarding right faith has occurred within this fortnight — all of that, by mind, speech and body, may my wrong-doing be undone. 1. First among the twelve is the vow of abstaining from injury to life: a resolve not to kill, with deliberate intent and formed purpose, innocent mobile beings from the two-sensed upward — with careful guarding in unavoidable activity. Now I examine its five transgressions: binding, beating, mutilating, overloading, withholding food and drink. If I have tied two-footed or four-footed creatures in tight bonds. If, in anger, I have dealt a heavy blow to cattle, dogs, cats, servants, children, offspring or calves. Mutilation — if I have cut an ear, dewlap and the like, branded with a ring, or nose-roped oxen. Overloading — if I have loaded an excessive burden on pack-bullocks, draught-beasts, camels, oxen, donkeys or mules. Withholding food and drink — if, as head of the household, I have failed to care for the hungry, thirsty, sick, aged, children and calves; made a debtor swear off food, forced fasting on others, flayed/over-driven (a beast), had verminous grain milled or coarse-ground, failed to give a water-strainer in time, taken no care about mould, set traps, killed any creature, set fire to a heap, left a lamp uncovered, plastered with stale dung myself or through others, dug soil in the rains, sunned bed-bugs (to death), bathed at night, cracked a whole areca-nut with my teeth, or failed to teach my household careful guarding. Whatever other subtle or gross transgression regarding this first non-violence vow has occurred within this fortnight — all of that, by mind, speech and body, may my wrong-doing be undone.
idiomaticTranslation: Willingly I take up the householder's path, whose root is right faith and whose body is the twelve vows. With all my heart I hold fast to right faith — the Arihant alone as my God, the true ascetic alone as my Guru, and the Dharma the Jina taught. Now let me name, that I may forsake it, the four-fold false belief. To have adored Hari, Hara, Brahmā, the Sun and Moon, the planets, clan-gods, Gaṇeśa, the world-guardians, Skanda, Kapila, the Buddha, Hanumān, yakṣas and rākṣasas as though they could grant release — that is worldly faith misplaced in gods. To have revered Carakas, wandering monks, Kaulas, Kāpālikas, brahmins and forest-ascetics as ferrymen across rebirth — that is worldly faith misplaced in teachers. To bow, as to true gods, before a Jina-image kept by outsiders, or before Vairoṭyā, Brahma, Śānti and the rest — that is misdirected faith in gods within religion itself. And to honour, as true gurus, the lax, the slack, the ill-conducted, the entangled, the self-willed, the doctrine-denier, the Boṭika, or one who is an ascetic in dress alone — that is misdirected faith in teachers. All four I now renounce as far as I am able. Next I search out the five flaws of right faith — doubt, craving, wavering disgust, the praise of alien creeds, and intimacy with them. Have I doubted any of the nine truths, or the way of God, Guru and Dharma? Have I hankered after another faith, or weighed all faiths as one and the same? Have I doubted whether dharma bears fruit, or curled my lip at an ascetic grimy with the dust of his austerities? Have I praised some rival creed for its dazzling learning and renown, or courted its followers with fond talk, affection, gifts and easy company? Of my own will have I gone to alien shrines, bathed and given and offered fire there, or kept the eclipse- and solstice-baths? Have I worshipped at Holī, poured water before pīpal and tulsī, bathed as worship in rivers and tanks, fasted for Ekādaśī, trooped after the crowd on some god's pilgrimage or fair, joined the tulsī-wedding, or feasted at a śrāddha? Setting aside the proper Jain days — the eighth, fourteenth, the moons, and Paryuṣaṇa's third, fourth and fifth — have I kept, as holy, the countless folk-festivals from New-Moon-day to Śiva's Night, and the eclipses and ill-starred conjunctions? Whatever else of the Jina's word I have broken this fortnight, subtle or gross — all of it, in thought, word and deed, may it be undone. And now the first vow: not to kill, with intent and resolve, any innocent moving creature from the two-sensed upward, guarding myself with care in life's unavoidable tasks. Its five slips I examine: binding, beating, maiming, overloading, starving. Have I bound man or beast in cruel ropes? Struck cattle, dog, cat, servant, child or calf in a fit of rage? Cut an ear or dewlap, branded, or pierced an ox's nose? Piled killing loads on bullock, camel, ox, ass or mule? As the family's provider, have I left the hungry, thirsty, sick, old, the children and the calves uncared for — forced a debtor to fast, starved another, milled worm-ridden grain, withheld the water-strainer, ignored the mould, laid traps, killed any creature, fired a refuse-heap, left a lamp open, plastered with stale dung, dug the rain-soaked earth, sunned the bugs to death, bathed by night, split a whole betel-nut in my teeth, or failed to teach my own house this careful way? Whatever subtle or gross slip in this vow has happened this fortnight — all of it, in thought, word and deed, may my wrong be undone.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary:
This opening movement does something the later vow-sections do not: before confessing any single vow it re-secures the ground they all stand on — samyaktva. The structure is deliberate. First the practitioner affirms the true triad (Arihanta–susādhu–jina-praṇīta-dharma), then maps its shadow, the four-fold mithyātva, along two crossed axes — laukika/lokottara and deva-gata/guru-gata — and fills each cell with names a medieval Gujarati householder would actually have met: Purāṇic gods and the Buddha under worldly deva-gata; Caraka, Kāpālika and brahmin under worldly guru-gata; even a Jina-image 'para-parigṛhīta' (held by outsiders) and the five kinds of fallen monk (pāsatthā, usannā, kuśīla, saṁsakta, ahācchaṁda) under the lokottara cells. The brilliance of the lokottara category is that the object can be perfectly orthodox — a Jina-bimba



a Jina-bimba, a recognized Jain devī — yet the *attitude* (deva-buddhe, guru-buddhe) is misdirected, so the slip lies in the worshipper, not the worshipped. The text then turns from belief to behaviour: the five named atichāras of samyaktva (śaṅkā, kāṅkṣā, vitigicchā, para-pāṣaṇḍī-praśaṁsā, para-pāṣaṇḍī-saṁstava), each glossed twice over — a doctrinal sense (doubting a tattva) and an everyday sense (feeling dugaṁchā at an ascetic's grime). A long, concrete catalogue of popular observances follows, climaxing in the named loka-parvas; crucially it is bounded by 'ṭāḷī' — the fault is not the calendar but keeping non-Jain days *parva-buddhe* while the proper Jain tithis are set aside. Only then, sealed by 'micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ', does the confession reach the first aṇuvrata. Here the canonical Prakrit pentad — bandhe, vahe, chavicchede, aibhāre, bhattapāṇavucchee — is quoted and unpacked into kitchen-and-byre realities: tight ropes, blows struck in rīsa (anger), nose-roping oxen, killing loads on pack-beasts, and the slow violence of neglect (no strainer, verminous grain milled, the household left untaught in jayaṇā). The verse thus moves root → faith-slips → worldly drift → first vow, establishing the template every later vow reuses.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse-specific teaching is that conduct (cāritra) is worthless without its root, darśana: hence the confession opens not with a vow but with samyaktva-mūla. Two points are sharpened here. (1) The four-fold analysis of mithyātva shows that false belief is not only worshipping the 'wrong' object but worshipping even a right object with a wrong intent — the lokottara cells make the *bhāva* decisive, aligning with the Jain axiom that karmic bondage tracks adhyavasāya (intention), not the external act alone. (2) The bounding word 'ṭāḷī' before the proper Jain tithis encodes the doctrine that observance is not rejected wholesale; rather, niḥśaṅkita right faith discriminates Jina-ordained days from loka-pravāha custom. In the first vow, 'sthūla… niraparādha… saṁkalpī' precisely delimits the lay aṇuvrata against the monk's mahāvrata: the householder forswears intentional killing of innocent mobile (trasa) beings, while ārambha-jā harm is governed by jayaṇā, not absolute abstention — the doctrinal hinge that makes lay non-violence livable yet binding.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited today, this verse asks the śrāvaka two honest questions before any vow-by-vow review: 'Where has my faith leaked?' and 'Where has my kitchen, my anger, my livestock, my calendar quietly cost lives?' The lokottara category is the sharp one for modern practitioners — it warns that even temple-going and devotion can become mithyātva if directed deva-buddhe at miracle-seeking rather than at the path. The non-violence catalogue translates cleanly: drive/transport animals without overloading, feed dependents (human and animal) on time, strain water, mind mould and pests without killing-by-convenience, and — explicitly — *teach the household* jayaṇā, since the gṛha-nāyaka bears the family's collective hiṁsā. The closing 'subtle or gross… mind, speech, body' reminds the reciter that intention and instigation count as much as the deed.

notes: This is by far the longest verse of the sūtra (samyaktva-mūla + the parva-catalogue + the first aṇuvrata); per-token coverage groups the canonical Prakrit atichāra-formulas and the long deity/festival/example enumerations item-by-item, while the recurring confessional auxiliaries (hoya, kīdhī/kīdho, dīdho, na hoya, the done/caused pair kīdhāṁ–karāvyāṁ) are glossed once at their first occurrence and recur identically thereafter. Several glosses on the medieval-Gujarati domestic vocabulary are reasoned from context and dialect and are flagged uncertain: 'kanyāhala', 'dhru-āṭhama', 'ahava-daśamī', 'khāla vāhyā', 'dhābā māṁḍyā', 'varāse', 'pakhocyā', 'paḍalī' — the book is authoritative and its spellings (incl. 'atiyār' અતિયાર, not atichāra) are preserved verbatim, including the footnote-bars (*) marking pāṭhāntare. Per the preface's substitution rule, every 'pakṣa-divasa-māṁhe' (this fortnight) re-times to 'these four months'/'this year' in the Cāumāsī/Sāṁvatsarī observances. 'Boṭika' is rendered per the Śvetāmbara sectarian framing of the text and is not an editorial judgement. Tattvārtha cross-references give the Sanskrit canonical forms of lists the text quotes in Prakrit; the medieval Gujarati glosses are this recension's own.
